reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (30% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [High risk] — 1 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 100/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  guardian (unknown): 0/100 [Critical risk] — no findings

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — score below 30: guardian (0/100)

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 100/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (high risk) — Δ50 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Sentinel scored 50/100 (high risk) while guardian scored 0/100 (critical risk) — Δ50 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from guardian to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 100/100 (info risk) while guardian scored 0/100 (critical risk) — Δ100 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • -15% confidence: 1 module(s) failed to complete
  • Base score: 75/100 average across 2 module(s)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
The two completed assessments converge on a single dominant issue: absence of source code and artefacts prevents concrete analysis. Sentinel (sentinel-1-56dfc9f0) and Watchdog (watchdog-1) both call out missing repository/files as the primary blocker, leaving core application controls—authentication, file-upload handling, database queries, dependency vulnerabilities, prompt templates or LLM integrations—unverified. Because of this, specific technical vulnerabilities cannot be tied to code paths and remediation steps remain high-level recommendations.

There is a notable conflict in risk framing: Sentinel flags a high residual risk for the web application given the unknowns, while Watchdog labels its findings informational because no LLM usage was detected in the supplied artefacts. Compounding this, Guardian returned a critical score but provided no findings or governance evidence, creating an unfilled gap in compliance and risk-management assessment. Immediate next steps are clear: provide the complete source repository and deployment manifests, perform code-level SAST and dependency/SCA scans, conduct cloud configuration and secrets reviews, and obtain a governance-focused NIST AI RMF mapping (including logging, incident response, data governance, and human-in-the-loop controls). If LLMs are added or are present but not shown, apply Watchdog's prompt-injection and tool-control mitigations immediately.

top_findings: Insufficient Source Code Provided for Security Analysis
sentinel.summary: VeriMedia AI is described as a Flask-based AI media verification platform with deep learning models, social media integration, and user-uploaded content processing — but no source code was provided for analysis. Sentinel cannot assess authentication, input validation, secrets management, file upload security, API security, or dependency vulnerabilities without actual code to review.
sentinel.recommendation: Submit the complete VeriMedia AI application source code for re-analysis. Prioritise Flask route definitions, file upload handlers, database queries, and third-party API integrations as these represent the highest-risk attack surfaces.

top_findings: No LLM/LLM-integration code detected in provided artefacts for VeriMedia AI
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI — scanned artefacts contained no accessible source files and no explicit LLM usage was detected in the provided application profile. The product uses ResNet and BERT for CV/NLP tasks (non-LLM). Without source code or file-level access, no concrete OWASP-LLM findings (e.g., OWASP-LLM01 prompt injection) could be identified or mapped to file paths.
watchdog.recommendation: Top 3 priority actions: 1) Provide the source files or repository (especially any modules that call OpenAI/Anthropic/Cohere, LangChain orchestration, prompt templates, RAG/retriever code, embedding storage and moderation/autonomy logic) so a line-by-line LLM risk assessment can be performed. 2) If you intend to add LLMs, implement prompt injection protections immediately: keep system prompts server-side, use parameterized prompt templates, output validators (JSON Schema/pydantic), and never conc
